1:
"And God spake all these words, saying," The witnesses note that God himself spake all the words of the law, the Ten Commandments. Gill notes these words which follow are commonly called the decalogue, or ten commands, a system or body of laws selected and adapted to the case of the people of Israel. The witnesses note that God spake all these words, the great law about to be given; the solemn opening of the giving of the Ten Commandments, spoken by God himself — that the law comes from the mouth of God: "And God spake all these words, saying"; the witnesses note the author, "God spake" — that these laws were uttered by God himself, with his own voice, from the midst of the fire and the cloud at Sinai, not delivered by Moses only nor by an angel, the dignity of the giver marking the weight of the law; the witnesses note the fulness, "all these words" — the whole Decalogue, the "ten commands," a complete "body of laws," the sum of the moral law; the witnesses note the abiding character of this law the commentators draw, that it is the eternal rule of righteousness, binding on all men, summed up by Christ in love to God and neighbour (Matt. 22:37-40); the witnesses note the awful solemnity of God's own voice proclaiming his law to the assembled people; the witnesses note that the law's coming from God himself binds the conscience to obey; so that God himself speaks the words of the law; And God spake all these words, saying. The witnesses note And God spake all these words. The witnesses note saying.

The witnesses note God himself speaking all the words of the law — the Ten Commandments, the sum of the moral law — with his own voice from the fire at Sinai, not by Moses or an angel; the dignity of the giver marking the weight of the law, binding the conscience to obey.

So the witnesses make the verse the solemn opening of the giving of the law. The lesson the expositors draw is that God himself spake all the words of the Decalogue, the eternal rule of righteousness, with his own voice, binding all to obey. The witnesses note the God spake: "God spake all these words" — Gill notes the decalogue; the God spake; the the decalogue; the ten commands; the the moral law; the spoken. The expositors observe the his own voice: with his own voice from the fire; the his own voice; the his voice; the the fire; the the cloud; the at Sinai. The witnesses press the not by Moses: not by Moses or an angel; the not by Moses; the not Moses; the nor an angel; the the giver; the the dignity. The expositors draw the lesson on the all these words: "all these words" — the whole Decalogue; the all these words; the the whole; the the Decalogue; the the body of laws; the the sum. The witnesses observe the the moral law: the eternal moral law; the the moral law; the eternal; the the rule; the righteousness; the binding. The expositors note the summed by Christ: summed by Christ in love; the summed by Christ; the Christ; the love; the to God; the and neighbour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And God spake all these words, the saying, and the giving of the law. The verse thus records the solemn opening of the law — teaching that God himself spake all the words of the Decalogue, binding all to obey.
2:
"I am the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage." The witnesses note the preface to the commandments, God's name and his redemption of Israel. Gill notes this verse does not contain the first of the commands, but is a preface to them, showing that God had a right to enact and enjoin the people of Israel laws. The witnesses note God prefacing the law with his name, the LORD their God, who had brought them out of Egypt and the house of bondage; the foundation laid for the law, God's authority and his redeeming love — that God first declares who he is before he commands: "I am the LORD thy God, which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage"; the witnesses note that this is "a preface" to the commands, not itself the first, "showing that God had a right to enact and enjoin" laws on Israel; the witnesses note the ground of his authority, "I am the LORD thy God" — that as Jehovah, the self-existent and almighty God, and their covenant God ("thy God"), he had the right to command, and they the duty to obey; the witnesses note the ground of his claim on their love and obedience, "which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage" — that he had redeemed them from cruel slavery, and so by the right of redemption they were his, bound to serve him; the witnesses note the gospel order the commentators mark, that redemption goes before the law, mercy before duty, the redeemed obeying out of gratitude; the witnesses note that God's people obey not to be saved but because they are saved; so that God's redeeming love is the ground of the law; out of the house of bondage. The witnesses note I am the LORD thy God. The witnesses note which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of bondage.

The witnesses note the preface to the law — God declaring who he is (the LORD their covenant God) and what he had done (redeemed them from bondage) before he commands; redemption going before the law, mercy before duty, the redeemed obeying out of gratitude, not to be saved but because they are saved.

So the witnesses make the verse the preface, God's authority and redeeming love grounding the law. The lesson the expositors draw is that God laid the foundation of the law in his name and his redemption, redemption going before duty, the redeemed obeying out of gratitude. The witnesses note the a preface: "I am the LORD thy God" — Gill notes a preface; the a preface; the not the first; the the preface; the his right; the to command. The expositors observe the the LORD thy God: "the LORD thy God" — Jehovah, their covenant God; the the LORD thy God; the Jehovah; the their covenant God; the the right; the to obey. The witnesses press the brought thee out: "brought thee out of... Egypt" — redeemed from bondage; the brought thee out; the from bondage. The expositors draw the lesson on the redemption before law: redemption before the law; the redemption before law; the redemption; the before law; the mercy before duty; the the order. The expositors note the not to be saved: obey because saved, not to be saved; the not to be saved; the the gospel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the which have brought thee out of the land of Egypt out of the house of bondage, and the preface. The verse thus records the preface to the law — teaching that God's authority and redeeming love ground the law, redemption going before duty.
3:
"Thou shalt have no other gods before me." The witnesses note the first commandment, forbidding all other gods. Gill notes this is the first command, opposed to the polytheism of the Gentiles, the Egyptians from whom Israel was just come, requiring that God alone be acknowledged and worshipped. The witnesses note the first commandment forbidding any other gods before the LORD; the foundation of all true religion, that God alone is to be owned and worshipped — that the first command claims God's sole right to be God: "Thou shalt have no other gods before me"; the witnesses note the matter of the command, that it forbids all "other gods," all the false deities of the heathen (especially the gods of Egypt, just left, and of Canaan), and requires that the LORD alone be acknowledged as God; the witnesses note the force of "before me" (or "before my face") — that to set up any other god is done in God's very sight and presence, an affront to him who sees all; the witnesses note the breadth of the command the commentators draw, that it forbids not only gross idolatry but all that puts anything in God's place in the heart, whether self, the world, riches, or pleasure, every idol of the soul; the witnesses note that this first command is the foundation of all the rest, for he who truly has the LORD for his God will keep all his commandments; the witnesses note that God will share his glory and worship with no other; so that God alone is to be owned as God; Thou shalt have no other gods before me. The witnesses note Thou shalt have no other gods. The witnesses note before me.

The witnesses note the first commandment — that God alone is to be owned and worshipped, all other gods forbidden — done "before his face," an affront to him who sees all; the command forbidding not only gross idolatry but all that puts anything in God's place in the heart, every idol of the soul.

So the witnesses make the verse the first commandment, God's sole right to be God. The lesson the expositors draw is that all other gods are forbidden and the LORD alone owned, the command reaching to every idol of the heart, the foundation of all true religion. The witnesses note the no other gods: "no other gods" — Gill notes against polytheism; the no other gods; the against polytheism; the the heathen; the Egypt; the Canaan. The expositors observe the God alone: God alone to be owned; the God alone; the alone; the owned; the worshipped; the the LORD. The witnesses press the before me: "before me" — in God's very sight; the before me; the in God's sight; the before his face; the an affront; the sees all. The expositors draw the lesson on the every idol: every idol of the heart; the every idol; the every idol; the the heart; the self; the the world. The witnesses observe the the foundation: the foundation of all the rest; the the foundation; the the foundation; the all the rest; the true religion; the the first. The expositors note the no other glory: God shares his glory with no other; the no other glory; the his glory; the no other; the his worship; the alone. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Thou shalt have no other gods, the before me, and the first commandment. The verse thus records the first commandment — teaching that God alone is to be owned as God, the command reaching to every idol of the heart.
4:
"Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth:" The witnesses note the second commandment, forbidding the making of images for worship. Gill notes thou shalt not make any image of anything graven by art or man's device, cut out of wood or stone, or anything molten, or cast into a mould. The witnesses note the second commandment forbidding any graven image or likeness of things in heaven, earth, or sea; the prohibition of all images in the worship of God — that the second command guards the manner of God's worship: "Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven above, or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth"; the witnesses note the breadth of the prohibition, "any graven image, or any likeness of any thing" — that no image at all, whether "graven" (carved of wood or stone) or molten (cast of metal), of any creature "in heaven... in the earth... in the water," might be made to represent or worship God; the witnesses note the reason the commentators give, that God, being a Spirit, infinite and invisible, cannot be likened to any creature, and to make an image of him is to dishonour and debase him; the witnesses note that the command forbids both the making of idols of false gods (with the first command) and, especially, the worshipping of the true God by images, the corrupting of his worship; the witnesses note that God will be worshipped as he has appointed, not after the inventions of men; so that all images in worship are forbidden; or that is in the water under the earth. The witnesses note Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image, or any likeness of any thing that is in heaven above. The witnesses note or that is in the earth beneath, or that is in the water under the earth.

The witnesses note the second commandment forbidding any image or likeness of any creature made to represent or worship God — for God, being a Spirit, infinite and invisible, cannot be likened to any creature; the corrupting of his worship by images forbidden, God to be worshipped as he has appointed.

So the witnesses make the verse the second commandment, forbidding images in worship. The lesson the expositors draw is that no image of any creature might be made to represent or worship God, who, being a Spirit, cannot be likened to anything made. The witnesses note the graven image: "any graven image" — Gill notes carved or molten; the graven image; the carved; the molten; the of wood; the of metal. The expositors observe the any likeness: "any likeness of any thing" — no image at all; the any likeness; the no image; the of any creature; the heaven; the earth. The witnesses press the cannot be likened: God cannot be likened to a creature; the cannot be likened; the God; the a Spirit; the invisible; the infinite. The expositors draw the lesson on the dishonours God: to image him dishonours him; the dishonours God; the dishonours; the debases; the his glory; the corrupts. The expositors note the as appointed: God worshipped as appointed; the as appointed; the not men's inventions. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the or that is in the earth beneath or that is in the water under the earth, and the second commandment. The verse thus records the second commandment — teaching that no image of any creature might be made to represent or worship God.
5:
"Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate me;" The witnesses note the forbidding of all worship of images, and God's jealousy. Gill notes thou shalt not perform any worship to them, show any reverence by any gesture of the body, bowing being put for all other acts of worship. The witnesses note God forbidding to bow down to or serve images, for he is a jealous God, visiting the fathers' iniquity on the children to the third and fourth generation of them that hate him; the prohibition of idolatrous worship and the awful reason of God's jealousy — that God guards his worship with a solemn threat: "Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God"; the witnesses note the act forbidden, "bow down thyself to them, nor serve them" — that no worship at all, neither outward gesture (bowing) nor inward service, might be given to images; the witnesses note the reason, "for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God" — that God, like a husband jealous for his wife's love, will not endure a rival in the worship and devotion of his people, but is jealous for his own honour; the witnesses note the threat, "visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children... of them that hate me" — that God would punish idolatry, that great sin and hatred of him, even visiting its effects on the children of the guilty who followed their fathers' sin, to the third and fourth generation; the witnesses note that idolatry is hatred of God, the breaking of the marriage-bond between him and his people; so that idolatrous worship is forbidden, God being jealous; unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate me. The witnesses note Thou shalt not bow down thyself to them, nor serve them: for I the LORD thy God am a jealous God. The witnesses note visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate me.

The witnesses note God forbidding all worship of images, for he is a jealous God who will not endure a rival in his people's devotion — visiting the fathers' iniquity on the children who follow their sin; idolatry being hatred of God, the breaking of the marriage-bond between him and his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the prohibition of idolatrous worship and God's jealousy. The lesson the expositors draw is that no worship might be given to images, for God is jealous for his honour and punishes idolatry, the hatred of him, even on the children who follow it. The witnesses note the bow down nor serve: "bow down... nor serve them" — Gill notes all worship; the bow down nor serve; the no worship. The expositors observe the a jealous God: "a jealous God" — endures no rival; the a jealous God; the his honour. The expositors draw the lesson on the visiting iniquity: "visiting the iniquity of the fathers" — punishing idolatry; the visiting iniquity; the the children. The expositors note the the marriage-bond: idolatry breaks the marriage-bond; the the marriage-bond; the the bond. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children unto the third and fourth generation of them that hate me, and the jealousy. The verse thus records the prohibition of idolatrous worship — teaching that God, jealous for his honour, punishes idolatry, the hatred of him.
6:
"And shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments." The witnesses note the promise of mercy to thousands of them that love God and keep his commandments. Gill notes God shows mercy to thousands of them that love him, and show their love by worshipping God, and him only, serving him acceptably with reverence and godly fear, by a cheerful obedience. The witnesses note God promising to show mercy to thousands of them that love him and keep his commandments; the gracious counterpart to the threat, the abundant mercy promised to the obedient — that against the threat to idolaters God sets a far greater promise to his lovers: "And shewing mercy unto thousands of them that love me, and keep my commandments"; the witnesses note the contrast the commentators mark, that the threat reached but to "the third and fourth generation" of haters, but the mercy to "thousands" (of generations) of lovers, God's mercy far outrunning his judgment; the witnesses note the persons, "them that love me, and keep my commandments" — that true love to God is proved by keeping his commandments (as Christ said, "If ye love me, keep my commandments," John 14:15), love and obedience joined; the witnesses note the abundance, "shewing mercy unto thousands" — that God delights to show mercy, and that to a vast multitude and through long generations, to all who love and obey him; the witnesses note the comfort, that the obedient and their seed are heirs of God's covenant mercy; so that God shows abundant mercy to them that love and obey him; them that love me, and keep my commandments. The witnesses note And shewing mercy unto thousands. The witnesses note of them that love me, and keep my commandments.

The witnesses note God's mercy to thousands of generations of them that love him — far outrunning the threat that reached but to the third and fourth; true love proved by keeping his commandments ("If ye love me, keep my commandments"), love and obedience joined.

So the witnesses make the verse the abundant mercy promised to the obedient. The lesson the expositors draw is that God shows mercy to thousands of generations of them that love and obey him, his mercy far outrunning his judgment. The witnesses note the shewing mercy: "shewing mercy unto thousands" — Gill notes the obedient; the shewing mercy; the the obedient; the mercy; the thousands; the abundant. The expositors observe the the contrast: the threat to the few, mercy to thousands; the the contrast; the the threat; the the few; the mercy; the thousands. The witnesses press the far outruns judgment: God's mercy far outruns his judgment; the far outruns judgment; the far outruns; the mercy; the judgment; the greater. The expositors draw the lesson on the love proved by obedience: "love me, and keep my commandments" — love proved by obedience; the love proved by obedience; the love; the proved; the obedience; the joined. The witnesses observe the if ye love me: "If ye love me, keep my commandments"; the if ye love me; the if ye love; the keep; the my commandments; the Christ. The expositors note the heirs of mercy: the obedient heirs of covenant mercy; the heirs of mercy; the heirs; the covenant mercy; the their seed; the the comfort. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And shewing mercy unto thousands, the of them that love me and keep my commandments, and the mercy. The verse thus records the abundant mercy — teaching that God shows mercy to thousands of them that love and obey him, far outrunning his judgment.
